headlines.title: Bay Area authorities urge vaccination to prevent measles outbreak
headlines.description: Bay Area health officials are urging parents and international travelers to follow vaccination guidelines to prevent measles infections over the summer as the United States grapples with a sharp increase in measles outbreaks that began in 2025. Measles is a highly contagious viral disease; in a room where one person is infected, nine out of 10 unvaccinated people will contract the disease. Symptoms can include fever, cough, runny nose, conjunctivitis, and a rash of flat, red spots. In 2025, the U.S. reported 2,289 measles cases, a tally higher than the combined total for the previous 25 years. This year, there have already been 2,260 confirmed infections. U.S. Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy Jr. has spoken publicly against vaccination, and at a news conference Tuesday, representatives of the San Francisco and Santa Clara County public health departments said that they are also encountering increased skepticism regarding the effectiveness of vaccines. Of the 52 confirmed cases this year in California, 49 were unvaccinated, or their vaccination status was unknown. In April, San Francisco reported two measles infections, marking the first cases in the city since 2019. Santa Clara and San Mateo counties have also recorded two cases each this year. “In our response to these two cases, we learned firsthand how resource-intensive a response to a single measles case can be,” said Dr. Farrell Tobolowsky, deputy director of the Communicable Disease Branch at the San Francisco Department of Public Health. “So, infections and outbreaks can put a real strain on public health, healthcare systems, and schools,” Tobolowsky added. Dr. Monika Roy, deputy health officer at the Santa Clara County Public Health Department, echoed Tobolowsky and emphasized that her team has been focusing on increasing vaccination rates to prevent outbreaks. “Having so many different cultures in our county means different languages,” said Roy. “So we need to be able to communicate to communities in ways that are not only accurate but culturally affirming.” Avoiding an outbreak is even more critical right now, as the local health agencies’ response might be hamstrung by federal funding cuts. When asked about her agency’s preparedness to deal with an outbreak, Tobolowsky said that if there are two or more cases simultaneously, the San Francisco Department of Public Health would have to call in additional staff to contain the spread. “We know that this is not the end of the funding cuts,” Tobolowsky said. “It is something that we are concerned about all the time, and is on our minds all day, every day.”

headlines.title: One dead, another hospitalized after San Jose house fire
headlines.description: One person died and another was hospitalized after a house fire early Wednesday in San Jose, according to the San Jose Fire Department. Firefighters responded to a working structure fire at a single-family home in the 1200 block of Sylvia Drive. The fire was contained to the home and did not spread to neighboring residences, firefighters said. One person was found dead at the scene. A second patient was taken to a hospital with injuries that were not immediately known. No other injuries were reported. Firefighters battled the fire for about 45 minutes. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. The American Red Cross is assisting three displaced adults and two dogs. Traffic in the area was impacted, and firefighters asked the public to avoid the area. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: San Francisco lowers speed limits near more schools to protect students
headlines.description: San Francisco will lower speed limits on more streets near schools after the city’s transportation agency approved Tuesday a new safety program aimed at reducing crashes and making it safer for children to travel to and from class. The San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency’s Board of Directors voted to establish a new default 20 mph speed limit on school streets and expand where 15 mph and 20 mph speed limits can be used, according to city officials. The changes were made possible by a new state law and will allow San Francisco to implement lower school speed limits years before they become the statewide standard in 2031. The first reduced speed limits will be installed before the start of the new school year near Dr. William L. Cobb Elementary School, Leonard R. Flynn Elementary School, St. Anthony-Immaculate Conception School, Jefferson Elementary School, Roosevelt Middle School and Ruth Asawa San Francisco School of the Arts, city officials said. Mayor Daniel Lurie emphasized the changes are intended to give parents greater confidence that their children can travel to and from school safely. “As families get ready for the new school year, they deserve to know that they’ll be sending their kids out to safer streets so they can get to class and back home, every day, without fear,” Lurie said in a statement released by the city. SFMTA Director of Transportation Julie Kirschbaum said the agency is moving quickly to take advantage of the new law so more families can benefit from calmer streets. “Nothing matters more than helping children get to school safely,” said Kirschbaum. “Every day, thousands of families walk, bike, roll, take Muni and drive to schools across San Francisco, and every one of those journeys should feel safe. Transportation officials said lower speeds significantly improve a pedestrian’s chance of surviving a crash. According to the agency, a person struck by a vehicle traveling at 20 mph has about a 90% chance of survival, compared with about 20% if the vehicle is traveling at 40 mph.

headlines.title: Half Moon Bay voters to decide farmworker housing project
headlines.description: The Half Moon Bay City Council on Tuesday approved a measure allowing the construction of an affordable housing complex for farmworkers. The five-story development, which would be built on Kelly Avenue, would include 40 units of affordable supportive housing for retired and older local farmworkers. Opponents, who argue the project is too tall, collected enough signatures to force the City Council to place the measure before voters. The measure will appear on the November ballot and requires a simple majority to pass. Gov. Gavin Newsom and other state leaders have criticized the city over how long it has taken to approve the project.

headlines.title: Family speaks out after mother, toddler killed in fiery, high-speed San Jose crash
headlines.description: In every photo on the Balistreri family’s wall, you see a smiling Ivana and her daughter, Lilly, a toddler who hadn’t yet turned 2. Now, the family is speaking out about the fiery December crash on Highway 87 in San Jose that took their lives, hoping to share their grief and serve as a cautionary tale for the community. The Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office has filed two counts of murder against the driver who struck them, Zachary Chernicky. Prosecutors allege he was driving more than 100 mph when he hit the family’s vehicle. An attorney representing the family said data from Chernicky’s car shows he had been driving more than 140 mph at times. The fatal collision occurred on Dec. 2 of last year. Ivana, Lilly, and Ivana’s fiancé, Zachary Martins, were on Highway 87 when traffic came to a halt. Martins was behind the wheel. “We were at a full stop,” Martins told NBC Bay Area. “I looked in my rearview mirror and I literally thought, is he gonna stop?” Chernicky did not stop. NBC Bay Area reviewed previously unreleased video of the incident. Following the high-speed impact, Martins jumped out of the burning car, desperately trying to save his fiancée and daughter. “Yeah, it’s blurry and vivid at the same time,” Martins said of his rescue attempts. “I was concussed and running off adrenaline.” Ivana and Lilly died inside the burning vehicle. San Jose Jul 17 San Jose Jul 16 San Jose Dec 4, 2025 The pain remains raw for the victims’ extended family. “They were rays of sunshine,” said Francesca Alexander, Ivana’s sister. “Ivana did not have a mean bone in her body. She was always very kind, very open.” Ivana’s mother, Benedettea Balistreri, mourned the loss of her granddaughter. “My little Lilly,” she said. “She was my sunshine.” Aurelio Balistreri, Ivana’s father, said the crash shattered their entire family. “He destroyed my life, my kids’ life, my wife’s life,” he said through tears. “Every morning I get up out of bed thinking about Zach not having his babies. That’s on my mind every day.” For Rosie Balisteri, Ivana’s sister, navigating the grief requires balancing overwhelming emotions just to make it through the day. “It’s hard,” she said. “Trying to hear her in my mind to go work out, go on a run or do something. But I just want to lie in bed and not do anything, so it’s just trying to find a balance and trying to keep her memory.” When asked about the possibility of forgiveness for the man accused of killing his family, Martins was resolute. “When we saw him in the courthouse, there was the tiniest, little microscopic piece of me that felt slightly bad for him cause I know prison is hell; it’s probably worse than death,” Martins said. “But then I got home and I realized I’m in [expletive] prison every day…I don’t think I ever will be [ready to forgive].” Benedettea echoed the desire for strict accountability. “I just want justice,” she said. “I just want the person responsible to spend the rest of his life in prison and think about what he did.” Through their heartbreak, the family is sharing their story with a singular hope: preventing another tragedy. “We just really want to bring attention to it, to make a plea to the community to slow down and think about getting home safe, the next person getting home safe, so we don’t have to go through something like this,” Alexander said. NBC Bay Area reached out to Chernicky and his attorney for comment but did not hear back.

headlines.title: Hundreds of illegitimate donation bins scattered across San Jose
headlines.description: Hundreds of donation bins in San Jose are misleading donors, operating without permission, and taking donations away from charities that serve the community, city leaders and local nonprofits said Tuesday. Goodwill Silicon Valley estimates there are at least 300 illegitimate bins scattered throughout San Jose. Goodwill said most are placed illegally in parking lots, near schools and on private property – collecting donations people believe are staying local. “We’ve got some bad actors out there that are trying to take advantage of our community,” San Jose Councilmember Michael Mulcahy said. City leaders said many of the bins aren’t connected to legitimate charities at all. “We started doing some research and realized that these are not authorized,” Mulcahy said. “I called property owners all over the city, the district that I represent, and nobody had given any permission to put these out.” Goodwill said many of the bins are operated by for-profit companies that sell donated items in bulk or overseas. The bins also often become magnets for illegal dumping, graffiti and overflowing trash. San Jose passed an ordinance years ago limiting donation bins and requiring permits, but officials said enforcement has lagged, allowing unapproved bins to multiply. They said it’s now unclear whether any permitted donation bins are even operating in the city, making it difficult for donors to know which bins they can trust. Goodwill is working with the city on possible solutions. In the meantime, Goodwill said if people are planning to donate, the safest bet is to take their items directly to a nonprofit’s staffed donation center or retail location.

headlines.title: Sausalito's city manager makes first court appearance since arrest
headlines.description: Sausalito’s city manager made her first court appearance Tuesday since her arrest last weekend. Elaine Forbes, the newly-hired city manager for Sausalito and longtime former director of the Port of San Francisco, faces misdemeanor charges of unlawful lodging, petty theft and prowling. Yacht captain Bill Aubrey discovered Forbes barricaded aboard the Lisa Marie at the Sausalito Yacht Harbor early Saturday morning after hearing her repeatedly blow the horn. He got in the vessel through a window and found Forbes barefoot and acting strange. “She just looked at me, put a smile on her face and said, ‘Good morning,'” Aubrey said. “And I said, ‘Good morning to you. How’s your day going?’ And she goes, ‘Good.’ And I said, ‘Why don’t you just have a seat right here?'” Aubrey called police, who said they had been looking for Forbes for days. She had been reported missing last week. Andy Cunningham said Forbes had been on board her boat, too. Cunningham said Forbes left it completely ransacked. “She pretty much ransacked the whole thing with clothing, jewelry, glassware, alcohol, salt, cherries, maraschino cherries,” Cunningham said. “She basically made a complete and utter disaster of the whole boat, throwing things this way and that.” Forbes was wearing Cunningham’s clothes when she was found and arrested, Cunningham said. sausalito Jul 20 sausalito Jul 19 Sam Singer, a Forbes family spokesman, said Forbes was suffering a mental health crisis. “Elaine had a mental breakdown,” Singer said. “She believed that she was on her boat, not somebody else’s boat.” Singer said Forbes is now hoping to get the help she needs. He said she wants to stay behind bars for now. “Elaine and her family are working to get counseling,” Singer said. “They’re speaking with a variety of different providers. They’re speaking with her attorney and they’re going to get her on the right path because she is a beloved, respected Bay Area public servant.” Forbes is scheduled to be back in court on Wednesday.

headlines.title: Bay Area union members stand in solidarity with striking C&H Sugar workers
headlines.description: Members of several unions across the Bay Area joined the picket lines Tuesday in solidarity with striking C&H Sugar workers. Trucks were backed up outside the Port of Oakland as longshore workers walked off the job to join picket lines at the C&H Sugar refinery in Crockett, standing in solidarity with other unions seeking what they call a fair contract. Around 90 Local 6 workers went on strike back on June 15. Since then, a ship carrying sugar has sat moving between Richmond and Crockett, waiting to be unloaded. NBC Bay Area’s Velena Jones has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: Record July Fourth crowds prompt investigation into San Francisco transit failures
headlines.description: Record-breaking crowds that overwhelmed San Francisco’s transit system and streets following the July Fourth fireworks show have prompted an investigation by the San Francisco Municipal Transit Agency. The SFMTA, which had urged the public to rely on mass transit for the holiday, admitted it was unprepared for the surge of passengers. Tens of thousands of people — including NBC Bay Area reporter Thom Jensen — were caught in the gridlock after flocking to buses, trains and Waymo autonomous vehicles. Judson True, SFMTA chief of staff and external affairs director, updated the board on Tuesday regarding the transit and traffic failures that stranded attendees into the early hours of July 5. “We are still very much working through our lessons learned for July Fourth. But the broad outlines are clear,” True said. Traffic and transit systems were simply overwhelmed by the volume of people, True noted. “We have some initial data,” True said. “We know the Muni system, as a whole, carried 41,000 more people than on the previous July Fourth. That’s about a 13% increase.” While the SFMTA does not regulate general traffic or autonomous vehicles in the city, True explained that the Muni system experienced severe delays caused by the heavy volume of cars on the streets, drivers illegally using bus lanes and breakdowns involving Waymo vehicles. “Based on our observations that night, we believe it took our shuttles about 90 minutes to make a trip we expected would take 30,” True said. The following day, Muni riders told NBC Bay Area that commutes took up to three times longer than usual, with some passengers forced to wait hours for buses and trains. “There’s only so much you can do before you kind of just accept your fate,” BART rider Gloria Zhu said. “It was also just push and shove into the trains. It was all just kind of you move with the people and hopefully get on a train before the last one is out.” The SFMTA apologized for the widespread delays but emphasized that all riders eventually returned home safely, crediting the dedication of Muni employees. True praised the workers “… who worked well into the morning on July 5 until every Muni customer who was waiting for a shuttle was safely transported.” City and transit officials are working to fix the logistical failures quickly ahead of Fleet Week, which returns in a couple of months and is expected to draw nearly a million visitors to the city. An SFMTA spokesman indicated that a more robust report addressing all of the holiday weekend’s transit issues will be released in two weeks.

headlines.title: Pleasanton-area wildfire burns more than 1,000 acres, 40% contained
headlines.description: Firefighters on Tuesday battled a fast-moving wildfire near Pleasanton that quickly spread to 1,007 acres, forcing hundreds of residents out of their homes amid dry conditions and soaring temperatures. Dubbed the Little Fire, the blaze broke out at about 3:40 p.m. near Vallecitos and Little Valley roads in the Sunol area. It quickly triggered evacuation orders for zones PLS-081 and PLS-103, according to Cal Fire. As of Wednesday morning, the fire was 40% contained. Fire officials said the forward rate of spread has been stopped, and crews continue to strengthen containment lines. Crews faced dry brush, steep terrain and limited access. Airborne crews with tankers and helicopters made water drops over the fire line as part of an aggressive aerial attack, while engines, ground crews and bulldozers fought the flames below. Multiple agencies from across the East Bay and South Bay, including the Alameda County Fire Department, joined Cal Fire in the effort. In the evacuated Ruby Hill gated community, fire engines staged directly next to residences. Firefighters used the backyards of some homes to reach the fire line behind the neighborhood, hiking in and out with heavy equipment to attack the blaze from the ground. “We have grass hilly areas that interfaces with neighborhoods … fast moving wildfire … but great work by firefighters on the scene,” said Marcus Hernandez, Cal Fire SCU unit chief. Hernandez credited defensible space around Ruby Hill for creating fuel breaks that helped protect properties. “Essentially a fuel break … great what they did behind that neighborhood there,” Hernandez added. “Aerial support really helped during this fire … five air tankers, three helicopters … great work by crews on the ground.” At this point, no homes have been damaged. However, one firefighter sustained moderate injuries and was taken to a local hospital. The firefighter has since been released. A charred hillside near the community illustrated how close the fire came to properties, underscoring why officials stressed the importance of evacuating when ordered. Law enforcement officers on the ground and in the air patrolled the neighborhood to assist with evacuations and ensure everyone safely left the area. For many residents, the exit was a frantic scramble as plumes of smoke drifted as far as Berkeley. Resident Hiresh rushed back to the neighborhood to rescue his mother after learning she was still inside the evacuation zone. “We live in a high fire area. You can see it. It’s dry all the time,” he said. “I actually came here when I found out about the evacuation order to help my mom because she was the only one here. I was worried it was going to cross Highway 84 and I’d be stuck, but when I saw it moving the other way, I was relieved and rushed here as fast as I could.” He and his mother quickly loaded their car with photos, clothes and other irreplaceable items before leaving. Matt Jones was also home when the evacuation orders were issued. “At that point I filled every water jug I had, got the dog food, and hit the road,” Jones said. Before fleeing, he made sure to check on friends. “Hey, do you have a way out? … I called my veteran buddy. His apartment needed to be checked on. That was on my list.” Despite the close call, Jones expressed faith in the crews working through the night. “I have confidence in those guys,” he said. Pleasanton resident Ande Hook, who photographed the fire in her neighborhood, narrowly escaped before officers arrived to clear the area. “It was just a little bit before the police came that I thought, you know what, maybe I should leave, and then the police came and I left,” Hook said. When asked what officers told her, she replied, “Leave. Why are you still in the house?” Many displaced residents sought refuge at an evacuation center set up by the city of Pleasanton at the Pleasanton Senior Center, located at 5353 Sunol Blvd., between Valley and Bernal avenues. Ruby Hill resident Geetha Ganesh was among the first to arrive. “Got the passports, water bottles and just got out,” she said. Despite the sudden departure, Ganesh remained hopeful. “Well, it was scary, but at the same time a little optimistic that it was not going to come into the community. Somehow they would be able to contain it as there was no wind.” Pleasanton Mayor Jack Balch, who lives in an evacuated area, visited the center after a scheduled city council meeting was canceled. He emphasized the dual threat of the fire and the weather. “Residents need to be safe, they need to take precautions to make sure they have water,” Balch said. “The heat is still an issue. I know we’re just under 90 degrees now, but it’s still pretty hot right now.” The city requested assistance from American Red Cross shelter workers. The organization said it will have two volunteers at the center Tuesday night and is preparing additional staff for the coming days in case a full-time shelter is needed. The city also has county emergency trailers on site and can convert the facility into an overnight shelter if necessary, according to the Red Cross. For those needing additional accommodations, the Alameda County Sheriff’s Office of Emergency Services stated temporary and emergency lodging resources are available through the Tri-Valley program at http://bit.ly/4fwSzw8. In downtown Pleasanton, locals monitored the wildfire as concerns about air quality grew throughout the day. “My wife had texted me earlier to say she noticed some smoke and could smell the smoke when the wind blows the smoke that side,” resident Ranjit Mavinkurve said. Nearby residents outside the evacuation zone watched the response closely. Jesus Rodriguez, who has lived in Pleasanton for two decades, said he was initially worried. “I was really concerned because it’s the first time I’ve heard of something like that happen,” Rodriguez said. However, after watching the response, he noted crews appeared to have a good handle on the blaze. “I think the crew is doing a really good job containing the fire,” he said. Meanwhile, local businesses stepped up to support the evacuees. Employees at Gay Nineties Pizza Co. prepared an order of 20 pies for the evacuation center. “We were more than happy, seeing the smoke, knowing about these evacuations, that we could do our part to help these people and feed them some good pizza,” employee Kevin Fried said. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.
headlines.source: Pete Suratos, Jocelyn Moran, Gia Vang and Jeff Ranieri

headlines.title: 1 dead after stabbing in parking lot of Fairfield business
headlines.description: One person died after being stabbed during an altercation in the parking lot of a Fairfield business late Tuesday morning, according to police. Police said they received multiple calls at about 10:50 a.m. about an altercation in the parking lot of a business along the 800 block of East Travis Boulevard. Witnesses told police that one person was stabbed during the altercation, police said. First responders transported the victim to the hospital, where they later died, according to police. The victim’s identity will be released following notification of next of kin. Additional information wasn’t immediately available. An investigation is underway.

headlines.title: 22-year-old man arrested for allegedly killing man in East San Jose
headlines.description: Editor’s note: An earlier version of this story identified the victim as female based on preliminary information from police. Authorities later said detectives determined the victim identified as male. This story has been updated. A 22-year-old man was arrested on suspicion of killing a man in East San Jose last week, police said Tuesday. Marco Jimenez, a San Jose resident, has since been booked into Santa Clara County Main Jail for murder in the killing of the unidentified man, who police say he knew. Officers first responded at about 12:59 a.m. Wednesday to a home on the 2100 block of Lanai Avenue to investigate a report of an unresponsive man, police said. Responding officers found the man unconscious and not breathing. Medical personnel tried to save him, but he died at the scene. At the time, the man had minor visible injuries, “but no obvious signs of fatal trauma were observed,” police said in a statement. Jimenez was detained at the scene and determined to be a person of interest, according to police. On Thursday, the medical examiner’s officer told detectives that the man’s manner of death was ruled as homicide, according to police. Detectives subsequently took Jimenez into custody. The motive and circumstances surrounding the homicide – San Jose’s 15th of 2026 – remain under investigation, police said. Anyone with information is urged to contact San Jose police Detective Sgt. Taylor or Detective Van Brande by email at 4257@sanjoseca.gov and/or 4542@sanjoseca.gov or by phone at 408-277-5283.
